confirmed.title: Empty-state fallback text hardcoded (table)
confirmed.detail: Visible fallback {emptyState ?? 'No records found.'} is a raw literal. The matching translation key table.empty ('No records found.') already exists in table.php but DataTable never calls t().
confirmed.why_real:
CONFIRMED — real user-facing missing-i18n defect.

Line 229 of packages-js/ui/src/table/DataTable.tsx renders `{emptyState ?? 'No records found.'}` — a raw, visible literal shown to end users in the table body when there are zero records. It is NOT developer-only (not console/test/data-attr); it is the actual empty-state text a user sees.

It is genuinely localizable but never localized:
1. The matching key already exists: packages/core/resources/lang/en/table.php defines `'empty' => 'No records found.'` and a pt_BR/table.php sibling exists. So `table.empty` is a complete, translated key with a non-English value waiting to be used.
2. The i18n plumbing is present and designed for exactly this case: packages-js/react/src/utils/useArqelTranslations.ts is documented as existing so `@arqel-dev/ui` primitives can "translate their own chrome (pagination, form actions, dialogs…)" via `t(key, fallback)`.
3. The primary consumer ResourceIndex.tsx already imports `useArqelTranslations` and calls `t('table.search.label', 'Search')` — proving the convention is in active use — yet it passes `emptyState={emptyState}` straight through untranslated. DataTable itself never imports or calls any t()/useTranslation (grep over the whole ui package and the file returned no i18n import). So in the common path where no `emptyState` prop is supplied, the hardcoded English literal is rendered and pt_BR users see "No records found." instead of the translated string.

The fix is trivial and idiomatic: `t('table.empty', 'No records found.')`, identical to the already-shipping `table.search.label` pattern. (Note: the adjacent "Loading…" literal at line 219 is the same class of gap but is outside this finding's scope.)

The line, file, and claim all match exactly.

confirmed.title: i18n.available is the raw config list, never validated against on-disk lang dirs → silent wrong-locale render
confirmed.detail: availableLocales() returns config('arqel.i18n.locales') verbatim (only filtering non-string/empty). The LocaleSwitcher renders one <SelectItem> per entry and the LocaleController allowlist check (in_array($locale,$available,true)) accepts any of them. But loadForLocale()/resolveLocale() silently fall back to defaultLocale() when the locale has no resources/lang/{locale} directory. Repro: set config('arqel.i18n.locales')=['en','pt_BR','es'] without shipping an 'es' lang dir. User selects 'Español' → POST passes validation, session/cookie persist 'es', app locale becomes 'es', but i18n.translations is the English fallback dictionary. The UI shows locale=es with fully English strings and no error. available should be intersected with locales that actually resolve on disk (or at least documented), so the switcher only offers loadable locales.
confirmed.why_real: Confirmed by reading the three relevant PHP files. (1) TranslationLoader::availableLocales() (lines 71-87) returns config('arqel.i18n.locales') verbatim, only dropping non-string/empty entries; it never intersects with on-disk resources/lang/{locale} dirs. (2) LocaleController::__invoke (lines 36-39) validates the POSTed locale with in_array($locale, $available, true) against that same raw list, then persists it to session('locale') and a forever cookie arqel_locale — so an unshipped locale like 'es' passes and survives. (3) HandleArqelInertiaRequests::i18nPayload() (lines 422-427) emits locale => app()->getLocale() (the raw 'es'), available => availableLocales() (raw list incl. 'es'), but translations => loadForLocale($locale). (4) loadForLocale → resolveLocale (lines 117-131) silently returns defaultLocale()'s dir when resources/lang/es is absent — a fallback the class docblock (lines 14-17) explicitly calls 'silenciosamente'. Net effect reproduces the finding exactly: the switcher advertises 'es', validation accepts it, app locale becomes 'es', but the shipped translations dictionary is the English fallback, yielding locale=es with fully English strings and no error. This is a genuine, user-facing wrong-locale render, not developer-only, not already guarded by any disk check, and the cited line matches the claim. The asymmetry between advertised `available` and actually-loadable locales is real and unhandled.

confirmed.title: i18n.locale comes from app()->getLocale() while available stays underscore-normalized → LocaleSwitcher Select can show empty value
confirmed.detail: i18nPayload() sets locale=app()->getLocale() and available=loader->availableLocales(). The two are not guaranteed to use the same separator convention. resolveLocale()/normalize() and SetLocaleMiddleware normalize '-'→'_', but app.locale config or a host that calls App::setLocale('pt-BR') directly leaves a hyphenated active locale, while availableLocales() returns whatever config holds (e.g. 'pt_BR'). On the client <Select value={locale}> compares against <SelectItem value={code}> from available; 'pt-BR' !== 'pt_BR' so no item matches and the Radix Select renders the placeholder instead of the current language. Repro: config app.locale='pt-BR' (or any hyphenated active locale) with arqel.i18n.locales=['en','pt_BR'] → switcher shows the placeholder, not 'Português (BR)'. PHP should normalize the emitted i18n.locale to match the available codes (or normalize both onto BCP-47).
confirmed.why_real: Confirmed at /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages/core/src/Http/Middleware/HandleArqelInertiaRequests.php:422. i18nPayload() sets `locale = app()->getLocale()` (raw, line 422/425) and `available = $loader->availableLocales()` (line 426). availableLocales() (TranslationLoader.php:71-87) returns the `arqel.i18n.locales` config values VERBATIM with no separator normalization. TranslationLoader::normalize() (line 137-139, `str_replace('-','_')`) is private and used only for on-disk lookup in resolveLocale(); it never touches the emitted i18n.locale nor the available list. The repro path is real: defaultLocale() (TranslationLoader.php:93-105) falls back to `app.locale` config UN-normalized, so a host with config app.locale='pt-BR' and arqel.i18n.locales=['en','pt_BR'] produces an active locale of 'pt-BR'. SetLocaleMiddleware (resolveLocale lines 48-70) returns defaultLocale() raw when no session/cookie/Accept-Language match, so App::setLocale('pt-BR') stands. i18nPayload then emits locale='pt-BR' with available=['en','pt_BR']. On the client (LocaleSwitcher.tsx:59-68) `<Select value={locale}>` ('pt-BR') is string-compared against `<SelectItem value={code}>` iterated from available ('en','pt_BR'); 'pt-BR' !== 'pt_BR' so no item matches and the Radix Select renders the placeholder instead of 'Português (BR)'. The DEFAULT_LABELS map even carries both 'pt_BR' and 'pt-BR' keys (lines 25-26), proving the JS authors knew separators diverge — but that only patches the label text, not the value/SelectItem match that Radix needs. Existing tests (HyphenatedLocaleTest) confirm hyphenated 'pt-BR' is a first-class accepted form. This is a genuine user-facing defect: the active-language indicator goes blank. The framework's own convention (SetLocaleMiddleware/resolveLocale on disk) is to normalize '-'→'_', but i18nPayload() and defaultLocale() fail to apply it to the emitted payload.

confirmed.title: LocaleController allowlist + SetLocaleMiddleware compare against un-normalized availableLocales(), so a hyphenated config locale is unreachable
confirmed.detail: Validation is in_array($locale,$available,true) with $available = loader->availableLocales() returned verbatim from config. SetLocaleMiddleware does the same strict comparison for session/cookie and for normalized Accept-Language candidates (it str_replace('-','_') the header but not the config list). If an operator configures arqel.i18n.locales with the hyphenated form ['en','pt-BR'] (a form the resolveLocale/DEFAULT_LABELS path explicitly claims to support, #250), then: (a) Accept-Language 'pt-BR' is normalized to 'pt_BR' and fails in_array against 'pt-BR'; and (b) resolveLocale normalizes 'pt-BR'→'pt_BR' and finds the pt_BR dir, so disk loading works but the negotiation/allowlist layer is comparing inconsistent forms. The allowlist and middleware should normalize both sides (config + candidate) before comparison so the hyphen/underscore variants are treated as one locale end-to-end.
confirmed.why_real: Confirmed against source. LocaleController.php:38 is `in_array($locale, $available, true)` with `$available = $this->loader->availableLocales()`. TranslationLoader::availableLocales() (lines 71-87) returns the configured `arqel.i18n.locales` VERBATIM with no hyphen/underscore normalization. Meanwhile resolveLocale()/normalize() (lines 110-139) WAS deliberately given `str_replace('-','_')` normalization so the hyphenated form `pt-BR` resolves to the `pt_BR` disk dir — and the docblock (lines 110-115) explicitly claims `pt-BR` is "aceite pelo allowlist" (#250). This creates the asymmetry the auditor flagged. Concrete reproduction with config `['en','pt-BR']`: SetLocaleMiddleware::parseAcceptLanguage (line 91) normalizes an `Accept-Language: pt-BR` header to `pt_BR`, then line 63 does `in_array('pt_BR', ['en','pt-BR'], true)` → FALSE, so negotiation silently falls through to defaultLocale() and the user's browser-requested pt-BR is never honored. The strict allowlist checks at lines 51/56/63 (middleware) and 38 (controller) compare un-normalized config against candidates that may be normalized, so hyphen/underscore variants are NOT treated as one locale end-to-end. The disk-loading layer (resolveLocale) and the negotiation/allowlist layer disagree. This is user-facing (locale negotiation behavior), not developer-only, not a test artifact, and not a false positive. Fix is to normalize both config side and candidate side before comparison.

confirmed.title: useArqelTranslations rebuilds the dict object every render → useCallback dependency [dict] changes identity each render, defeating memoization
confirmed.detail: dict is computed inline as a fresh object/reference on every render (the ?? {} branch and the cast produce a new value identity when translations is absent, and even when present the surrounding expression is re-evaluated). useCallback([dict]) therefore sees a new dependency each render and returns a new t function every time, so any consumer using t in its own dependency arrays (effects/memo) re-runs every render. Repro: mount a component that lists [t] in a useEffect dep array under an Arqel page — the effect fires on every parent re-render. Memoize dict with useMemo keyed on the i18n prop, or depend on i18n.translations directly.
confirmed.why_real:
Verified at /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages-js/react/src/utils/useArqelTranslations.ts:27-40. The line matches the claim: `dict` is computed inline (lines 27-30) and passed as the sole `useCallback` dependency `[dict]` (line 39).

The plumbing defect genuinely reproduces in one path: when `i18n.translations` is absent (non-Arqel pages, or a missing-i18n page), the `?? {}` branch (line 29) and the `: {}` branch (line 30) each evaluate to a FRESH empty object literal on every render. Since `{} !== {}` by identity, `useCallback([dict])` sees a new dependency every render and returns a NEW `t` function every render. Any consumer listing `[t]` in a useEffect/useMemo dependency array under such a page would re-run on every parent re-render. This is a real, verifiable memoization defect on the cited line.

Caveat / partial refutation of the auditor's wording: the claim that the identity changes 'even when present' is FALSE. When `translations` IS present, `dict` is the actual `i18n.translations` reference from Inertia `page.props`, which is stable across non-navigation re-renders, so `useCallback` correctly returns the same `t`. So the defect is narrower than stated — it only triggers in the i18n-absent fallback path, and the in-repo consumers (@arqel-dev/ui chrome) call `t()` inline in render rather than depending on `[t]`, so there is no demonstrated concrete user-facing regression today.

Net: the core plumbing bug (fresh `{}` literal in the dep array defeating `useCallback` identity stability) is real and reproduces on the cited line, matching the plumbing-bug dimension. The recommended fix (wrap `dict` in `useMemo`, or depend on `i18n.translations` / the i18n prop directly) is correct. Confirming, but with med confidence because the impact is latent (fallback-path only) and the auditor's 'even when present' rationale is inaccurate.

refuted.title: Action menu trigger aria-label hardcoded (mobile sheet)

However the finding does not represent a real, isolated defect, for two reasons. (1) It is NOT "genuinely not localizable": the monorepo ships a working i18n layer (packages-js/i18n with I18nProvider + useTranslation), so the string could be wired through translation if that were the package's policy. (2) It is one instance of a package-wide, intentional convention rather than a singular bug. Every aria-label across the entire ui library is a hardcoded English literal: ChartCard "Loading chart", FlashToast "Dismiss", Breadcrumbs "Breadcrumb", sidebar "Toggle Sidebar", DataTable "Select all rows"/"Actions", TableToolbar "Bulk actions", TablePagination "Pagination", CommandPalette "Commands", StatCard "Trend sparkline" — and notably the IDENTICAL `aria-label="Actions"` appears unflagged four lines above at line 115 (the desktop dropdown trigger). The primitive UI library deliberately ships default English aria-labels and does not consume the i18n hook. Singling out line 150 while the same literal sits unflagged at line 115 plus ten sibling labels confirms this is the established design convention of a headless/primitive component library, not a reproducible missing-i18n defect on this specific line.

refuted.title: Field placeholder and helperText serialized verbatim
refuted.why_refuted: Lines 186/191 of packages/fields/src/Field.php match the claim (getPlaceholder/getHelperText return raw strings), and FieldSchemaSerializer.php:83-84 emits them verbatim with no __() call. However, this is not a defect. The identical treatment is applied to 'label' (serializer line 78, raw getLabel(), no __()), confirming the framework's deliberate design: these are developer-supplied UI strings delivered faithfully to the React layer. This is the idiomatic Laravel localization pattern — the developer passes a resolved translation via ->placeholder(__('fields.x.placeholder')) and Laravel's __() resolves it transparently before it ever reaches the framework. The strings ARE fully localizable through this standard mechanism. Calling __() inside the serializer would be an anti-pattern: it would re-translate already-resolved strings and break literal (non-key) strings. The finding's complaint ('cannot be localized unless the app pre-resolves a translation key itself') literally describes how Laravel i18n is supposed to work; it is not a missing-i18n bug. No plumbing defect reproduces (the value set is the value delivered), and the strings are genuinely localizable. Refuted.

refuted.title: Select option labels (the value side of the {key:label} map) serialized raw
refuted.why_refuted: Line 351 and the serialize-as-is mechanism are accurately described: getTypeSpecificProps() emits 'options' => resolveOptions() (line 351), resolveOptions() returns staticOptions verbatim (line 178), and there is no __()/trans()/Lang call anywhere in SelectField.php. So the factual plumbing is correct. However, this is NOT a defect. Option labels are app-supplied display strings passed through the public options() API (line 67), which accepts arbitrary strings or a closure. The developer can and should localize at declaration time: options(['draft' => __('Draft'), 'published' => __('Published')]) or via a closure. This is the standard Laravel/Filament/Nova pattern. The framework correctly emits labels byte-for-byte because it cannot distinguish a translation key from already-final display text — auto-wrapping every label in __() would be actively wrong (double-translating already-localized strings, and turning legitimate literal labels into failed lookups). The finding itself concedes labels have 'no localization opportunity at the framework level,' which is exactly why this is correct framework behavior rather than a bug: localization is a declaration-time developer responsibility, and the opportunity fully exists at that layer. Refuted as a false positive / correct-by-design.

refuted.title: SelectColumn (editable) option labels serialized raw
refuted.why_refuted: Line 117 matches the claim verbatim: toArray() ships 'options' => $this->resolveOptions(), and no __() pass is applied. The labels do reach a user-facing inline-edit select. However, the missing-i18n bar is not met because the labels are NOT "genuinely not localizable." The options() method (line 34) accepts an arbitrary developer-supplied array OR a Closure; the developer fully controls every label string and can pass already-translated values, e.g. options(['a' => __('Active')]) or a Closure resolving translation keys. There is zero framework barrier to localization. This is the standard app-responsibility content pattern used consistently across the framework — SelectField (packages/fields/src/Types/SelectField.php) and every other option-label surface pass developer-supplied labels through as-is and never call __() anywhere in packages/table/src or packages/fields/src. Auto-translating arbitrary developer data literals would be incorrect/surprising behavior (it would feed unbounded data values into the translator). The framework correctly ships the structure while the app supplies localized content. This is a false positive (app-responsibility / not-a-bug), not a framework defect.

refuted.title: Filter::$label serialized verbatim
refuted.why_refuted:
The finding accurately describes the code (toArray() line 191 emits 'label' => $this->getLabel(); getLabel() at line 98-101 returns the developer-supplied label() or the constructor auto-titlecase at line 45). However the "missing-i18n" conclusion is a false positive.

The framework's translation hook IS the public label() setter (line 53), which accepts an arbitrary string. The idiomatic Laravel pattern is Filter::make('status')->label(__('filters.status')) — __() resolves at call time and the translated string is stored and serialized. The framework correctly does NOT hardcode __() internally because that would force a fixed translation key and prevent the developer from controlling it.

This exact pattern is the framework-wide convention: Field (packages/fields/src/Field.php:181-183) and Column (packages/table/src/Column.php:228-230, serialized at 387) implement getLabel() identically. None call __() internally — by design. The auto-titlecase at line 45 is only a sensible default when no explicit label is provided (matching Filament/Nova behavior), not a barrier to localization.

The string is user-facing but it IS localizable via the existing label() hook, so it is not "genuinely not localizable." Refuted.

refuted.title: SelectFilter option labels serialized raw
refuted.why_refuted:
REFUTED. The flagged code at packages/table/src/Filters/SelectFilter.php (normalizeOptions at line 128/134, shipped in getTypeSpecificProps at line 147) does cast app-declared option labels to string without wrapping them in __(). However, this is not a genuine defect for three reasons:

1. The labels ARE localizable. They are app-declared static strings passed via ->options(['active' => 'Active']). Nothing prevents the app from passing __('...')-translated strings: ->options(['active' => __('messages.active')]). normalizeOptions casts whatever scalar it receives, so translation happens at declaration time, exactly as Laravel apps normally do. There is no framework barrier to localization.

2. It is the established, consistent framework convention. A grep across all packages' src/ shows trans()/__()/Lang:: is used ONLY in auth and validation contexts (LoginRequest, ResetPasswordController, ScopedUnique, ResourceController), never in the field/column/filter serialization layer. The Filter base class itself ships its OWN label raw at Filter.php:191 ('label' => $this->getLabel()) with no __() wrapping. SelectFilter's option labels being raw is identical to how every other user-facing label in the serialization layer is handled.

3. Because the sibling Filter::getLabel() has the identical behavior by design, this is intentional architecture (app owns label strings, app localizes), not an oversight specific to this line. Wrapping app-supplied strings in __() inside the framework would be incorrect — these are not translation keys the framework owns.

The string is user-facing but genuinely localizable by the app, which is the refute condition for a missing-i18n claim. No SelectFilter/MultiSelectFilter-specific defect exists.

refuted.title: Table emptyState heading/description serialized verbatim
refuted.why_refuted: The line claim matches the file: emptyState() (Table.php:196) stores heading/description/icon raw and serialize() (lines 514-518) emits them verbatim with no __()/trans(). However, this is NOT a framework defect. The emptyState values are APP-SUPPLIED literals passed by the developer (e.g. ->emptyState('No records found')). This is the standard admin-panel contract: the framework treats all developer-supplied display strings as opaque literals and forwards them as-is. The exact same treatment is applied to the far more prominent Column::label() (table column headers, Column.php:387), which is also serialized raw. The framework DOES use __() — but exclusively for FRAMEWORK-ORIGINATED strings (auth flash messages, FormRequest validation messages in packages/auth and packages/core controllers, 14 occurrences). Wrapping an arbitrary app literal in __() would be semantically wrong: __('No records found') only resolves if the app defined a translation key literally named that string, otherwise it is a no-op. The correct localization path is for the developer to pass __('messages.empty') at the call site, which the current plumbing fully supports. No broken plumbing, no inconsistency, and the label IS localizable by the caller. Matches Laravel/Filament/Nova conventions.

refuted.title: Form Section heading/description serialized verbatim
refuted.why_refuted: Line 103-114 matches the claim verbatim: getTypeSpecificProps() emits 'heading' => $this->heading (line 106) and 'description' => $this->description (line 107) with no __()/trans(); heading is a required ctor arg (line 33). However this is NOT a missing-i18n defect. The heading/description are developer-supplied strings passed by the app author when composing the form layout (Section::make('...'), ->description('...')). The framework correctly does not auto-translate strings it doesn't own; the Laravel-idiomatic pattern is for the caller to wrap them: Section::make(__('forms.section')). They are fully localizable that way, so they fail the 'genuinely not localizable' bar required to confirm. This is the established, consistent convention across the whole layer: there are ZERO __()/trans() calls in the entire form/ and fields/ packages, and the canonical user-facing label — Field::label() (fields/src/Field.php:77-79, :183) — likewise stores and returns a raw developer string verbatim. So there is no framework-level localization step that is 'missing'; the verbatim pass-through is intentional design, identical to how every label in Arqel (and Filament/Nova) works. False positive.

refuted.title: Form Tab $label serialized verbatim
refuted.why_refuted: The line matches the claim: Tab.php:76 ships 'label' => $this->label (a required constructor arg, line 26/29) verbatim in getTypeSpecificProps() with no __()/trans(), and the label is genuinely user-facing (rendered as the tab caption in the React FormTab component). However, this is NOT a defect — it is the established, idiomatic Laravel design across the entire framework. The label is fully localizable BY THE CALLER: a developer wanting translated captions simply passes Tab::make('profile', __('tabs.profile')). I verified this is the consistent convention: NO label-bearing component in packages/form/src/Layout/ or packages/fields/src/ calls __()/trans() internally; the only __() usages in the codebase are in auth/validation/controllers for server-generated messages, never in the field/layout label pipeline. Calling __() internally on a developer-supplied label string would be the actual bug — it would force translation-key semantics onto every plain literal label and break the common case. Because the string is trivially translatable at the call site (and intentionally so, matching every sibling component), there is no missing-i18n defect. The finding mischaracterizes a deliberate, correct design as a bug.

refuted.title: Resource label / pluralLabel / navigationGroup feed navigation & page titles untranslated
refuted.why_refuted:
The file/line claims are factually accurate: Resource.php:78 getLabel(), :86 getPluralLabel(), :98 getNavigationGroup() return the raw static $label/$pluralLabel/$navigationGroup or an auto-titlecase of the model class, and these flow untranslated into the sidebar/page-title chrome via HandleArqelInertiaRequests.php:234,241 and InertiaDataBuilder.php:363-366 (all verified). The strings are genuinely user-facing.

But the "genuinely not localizable" bar is NOT met, so this is refuted:

1. These are non-final `public static` methods. The documented (Filament/Nova-style) extension path is for a developer to override `getLabel()`/`getPluralLabel()`/`getNavigationGroup()` to return `__('resources.user.label')`. That override runs per request under SetLocaleMiddleware, so the labels ARE localizable per active locale. The finding's core assertion ("cannot be localized despite SetLocaleMiddleware being wired") is false.

2. There is no framework i18n convention being violated. Field labels (InertiaDataBuilder.php:128 getLabel()/ucfirst fallback) are ALSO passed through raw with no auto-__(). The framework's __() calls are reserved for framework-owned fixed strings (flash messages: ResourceController __('arqel::messages.flash.*'); table/form chrome). Developer-supplied per-resource labels — both field labels and resource labels — consistently delegate localization to the developer. Resource labels behave identically to field labels, so there is no inconsistency or plumbing gap.

3. The auto-titlecase of the model class name is a sensible default fallback, not a localization defect. The absence of an automatic `__('arqel::resources.{slug}')` lookup is a design choice / arguable feature request, not a missing-i18n bug that renders the strings un-localizable.

Verdict: false positive — feature-request framed as a defect; the strings are localizable via getter override, consistent with how all other developer-supplied labels work in the framework.

refuted.title: validationAttribute serialized raw — error messages show untranslated attribute name
refuted.why_refuted: The plumbing described is accurate but it is not a defect. `validationAttribute(string $attribute)` (HasValidation.php:131) is a developer-facing override that stores a raw app-supplied string; `getValidationAttribute()` (line 197) returns it verbatim, the serializer emits it as `attribute` with no __() (FieldSchemaSerializer.php:103-108 — actually in packages/core/src/Support/, NOT the packages/fields path the finding cited), and FieldRulesExtractor::extractAttributes (packages/form/src/FieldRulesExtractor.php:88) copies it raw into the attributes map. Passing this string through unmodified is the CORRECT Laravel convention: it is the exact analog of FormRequest::attributes() / Validator setAttributeNames(), which Laravel itself substitutes into :attribute verbatim and never re-translates. The idiomatic way to localize is for the app to pass validationAttribute(__('validation.attributes.email')); re-applying __() inside the framework would be double-translation and wrong. The property defaults to null, in which case the serializer emits null, the extractor omits the field, and Laravel falls back to its native attribute resolution (validation.attributes lang keys / snake-cased name) client- and server-side — so the 'never falls back to a label, doubly disconnected' framing describes the absence of an extra opinionated feature, not broken behavior. No API, docblock, or Laravel convention promises auto-translation of this override, so nothing is broken. False positive (developer-controlled opt-in value correctly passed verbatim); the finding also misattributes the serializer file path.

refuted.title: Social-login sr-only labels hardcoded English (login form)
refuted.why_refuted: The cited lines match literally: login-form.tsx line 64 "Login with Apple", line 73 "Login with Google", line 82 "Login with Meta", all sr-only spans serving as the icon-only social buttons' sole accessible names. But this REFUTES rather than confirms a real defect. The file is a verbatim, unmodified shadcn/ui scaffold template: EVERY string is a hardcoded English placeholder ("Welcome back", "Login to your Acme Inc account", "Email", "Password", "Forgot your password?", "Or continue with", "Sign up", "Terms of Service"), it references the demo brand "Acme Inc", all links are dead href="#", and the image is /placeholder.svg. There is no i18n plumbing (t(), __(), react-i18next, Inertia translation props) anywhere in this file or the shadcn registry — by design, since shadcn components ship as copy-and-customize scaffolds. Singling out only the three sr-only spans is arbitrary: the equally-hardcoded visible labels (Welcome back, Email, Password) would be the same alleged violation. The auditor itself concedes "This is a shadcn scaffold template." A scaffold/registry template with placeholder English literals is not a user-facing i18n/a11y defect — these buttons are not a wired production login form, and the package follows shadcn's English-literal convention intentionally. This is a cosmetic false positive, not a reproducible plumbing or label-gap bug.

refuted.title: Social-signup sr-only labels hardcoded English (signup form)
refuted.why_refuted: Lines 73/82/91 do contain the literal sr-only spans "Sign up with Apple/Google/Meta" as claimed. However, the finding's framing as a real, distinct a11y-i18n defect is refuted by context. The file packages-js/ui/src/shadcn/signup-form.tsx is the verbatim, unmodified shadcn/ui "signup-form" block scaffold — a registry/demo template meant to be copied and customized. EVERY string in it is hardcoded English: the <h1> "Create your account", "Email", "Password", "Confirm Password", "Create Account", "Or continue with", "Already have an account? Sign in", and placeholder href="#". The component imports nothing translation-related (only cn + shadcn primitives) and exposes no i18n hook. The three sr-only spans are therefore indistinguishable from the dozens of other hardcoded visible strings in the same file — they are not specially un-localizable, and singling them out is selective. Hardcoded English copy in a shadcn scaffold template is expected and correct by design (it is starter code the consuming app edits). It is not a runtime Arqel UI element bound by a localization contract. No plumbing defect, no untranslatable accessible-name regression beyond the inherent nature of a demo block — false positive as framed.

refuted.title: Neither translate() implementation handles Laravel pipe '|' pluralization, silently returning the raw pluralized template
refuted.why_refuted: The code observation is factually accurate: in /home/diogo/PhpstormProjects/arqel/packages-js/i18n/src/translate.ts, buildTranslator() (line 36-41) returns interpolate(found, params), and interpolate() (line 26-34) only replaces :placeholder tokens via /:([a-zA-Z0-9_]+)/g. There is no pipe '|' / choice / count handling, so a 'singular|plural' template would indeed be returned verbatim with the pipe intact. HOWEVER, this does not meet the bar for a real user-facing defect: (1) The public contract TranslateFn (types.ts) is `(key, params?: Record<string, string|number>) => string` — it never advertises a `count`/`choice` parameter or trans_choice-style pluralization. The API never promised this capability. (2) The finding itself concedes 'Currently no shipped string uses |, so it is latent' — zero shipped strings are affected. (3) There is no README/doc claiming JS-side pluralization support, so there is no broken promise. (4) The 'repro' (t('messages.items',{count:2})) only fails against a hypothetical app-authored plural string the API was never designed to handle — i.e., app-misuse of an API that does single-key + placeholder substitution by design, mirroring Laravel __() (not trans_choice()). This is a missing-feature / documentation request ('either implement trans_choice-style selection OR document that pluralization is unsupported'), not a reproducible defect in any delivered functionality. A plumbing-bug verdict requires the defect to genuinely reproduce against actual shipped usage; it does not.

refuted.title: Two divergent dictionary shapes shared as separate props (flat 'translations' vs nested-by-file 'i18n.translations') invite key-prefix mismatches
refuted.why_refuted:
Verified against the actual files. The two props do exist: share() (L87-88) emits a FLAT `translations` (translations() L434, only the `arqel` group via load($locale,'*','arqel')) and a NESTED `i18n.translations` (i18nPayload() → loadForLocale(), keyed by file: arqel/messages/actions/table/form). The active runtime readers do consume only i18n.translations — I18nProvider.tsx L58 reads props.i18n, and useArqelTranslations.ts L26-29 reads props.i18n.translations. So the descriptive plumbing facts are accurate.

But this does NOT meet the bar for a confirmed plumbing-bug, because no defect actually reproduces:
- Nothing in the framework reads the flat props.translations at runtime. The finding itself concedes it is "dead weight" and frames the harm in purely hypothetical terms ("anything that reads props.translations ... WOULD miss", "invite", "footgun"). A redundant/unused prop with a divergent shape is dead code, not a reproducing user-facing bug.
- The translate() doc comment in react/src/utils/translate.ts L57-58 ("Apps build the dictionary from props.translations") is stale/misleading, but translate(dict, key) is shape-agnostic and no framework code wires the flat prop into it. useArqelTranslations.ts L7-8 documents the correct source (props.i18n.translations). So there is no fallback-to-key regression occurring in framework code.
- The string at L434 is not a user-facing label; it is developer plumbing.

This is a legitimate code-smell / cleanup candidate (consolidate on i18n.translations, drop or align the flat prop, fix the stale doc comment), but the claimed defect does not genuinely reproduce. Per the refute-by-default standard, it is refuted as a reproducing user-facing bug.

refuted.title: I18nProvider available defaults to [fallbackLocale] only, so a host without shared i18n shows a single-entry switcher even though more lang dirs exist
refuted.why_refuted:
REFUTED. The file at L33-38 does contain the described default fallback (`{ locale: fallbackLocale, available: [fallbackLocale], translations: {} }`), and the line matches the finding. But the claimed defect — a mid-session collapse where an Inertia partial reload drops `i18n` and blanks the switcher — does not reproduce, because it rests on two false premises about Inertia's plumbing:

1. The claim calls `'i18n' => fn () => $this->i18nPayload()` (L88 of HandleArqelInertiaRequests.php) a "lazy prop." It is not. In Inertia/Laravel a bare `fn()` closure in `share()` is an EAGER, always-evaluated prop. Lazy/optional props are `Inertia::lazy()`/`optional()`/`defer()`. A plain closure is included on every response — including partial reloads — unless explicitly named in `except`. So `only:[...]` omitting 'i18n' does not stop the server from being capable of sending it the way a true lazy prop would.

2. More decisively, Inertia's client MERGES partial-reload props into the existing page props. Props not listed in a partial reload's `only:[...]` are RETAINED from the prior page object, not deleted. Therefore `usePage().props.i18n` does not become `undefined` after a partial reload that omits it; the previously-resolved payload persists. The provider re-reading `usePage()` would still see the prior `i18n`.

The L33-38 fallback only fires when `i18n` is genuinely absent — the documented non-Inertia / test-host path (per the JSDoc at L10-25 and L53-60), which the finding itself concedes is intended. The "transient partial reload collapses available to one locale" scenario is a false-positive built on a misunderstanding of Inertia partial-reload semantics. The provider already correctly reads through `t()`/`buildTranslator`; the switcher-label-falls-back-to-raw-key consequence only manifests in the legitimately-mocked test host, not in a real running app.
